Q1.evidence:
Fixture (facts v2, one sample `work` @ /app/x.rb, value 7): /Users/logan_martel/Projects/autoclanker_bootstrap_v5/clankerprof-v2-audit/.goalloop-support/triage6/Q1/facts.json; configs slices_gc.yml (`- name: "(gc)"` / `paths: [/app]`), slices_unc.yml (same with `(uncollapsible)`), control slices_app.yml.

Commands (from worktree root, Q=.goalloop-support/triage6/Q1):
1) `uv run clankerprof slices --facts $Q/facts.json --slices $Q/slices_gc.yml` -> exit 0, stdout: {"slices": [], "summary": {"matching_pct": 100.0, "matching_time_ns": 7, "total_time_ns": 7}, "tool": "clankerprof_slices"} — no `gc` key, no slice row; the 7ns of matched time is attributed to nothing.
2) Same with slices_unc.yml -> exit 0, identical erased output (no `uncollapsible` key either).
3) `cargo run -q -p clankerprof-core --bin clankerprof-rs -- slices --facts $Q/facts.json --slices $Q/slices_gc.yml` -> exit 0, byte-identical erased output; ditto for slices_unc.yml.
4) Control (`name: app`) -> both languages emit the expected slice row: name "app", time_ns 7, pct 100.0, frame work//app/x.rb.
5) Duplicate-name control shows the existing validation pattern the fix should mirror: both languages print {"error": "Slice config declares duplicate slice name: app. ...", "ok": false} and exit 2.

Mechanism (code): clankerprof/slices.py:344-358 validates multiple-defaults and duplicate names only — no reserved-name check; slices.py:404 (`slice_name = matcher.slice_for_sample(...)`) attributes the ordinary sample into stats_by_slice under the user's `(gc)`/`(uncollapsible)` name; render.py:912-914 then unconditionally strips any row named `(gc)` or `(uncollapsible)` from `selected_slices`, and the real pseudo-outputs are gated on `result.gc_time_ns != 0` / `result.uncollapsible is not None`, both zero/None here (render.py:998-1005). Rust mirrors it exactly: crates/clankerprof-core/src/slices.rs:202-222 (validation without reservation), :259 (`slice_for_frame` attribution), :306-310 (render filter on GC_PSEUDO_SLICE/UNCOLLAPSIBLE_PSEUDO_SLICE).

Contract: docs/CLANKERPROF_SPEC.md "Slice projection contract" (lines ~540-580) promises per-sample ownership — "slices answers: 'after filters and collapse rules, which responsibility area owns this sample?'", "use the configured default slice or `(all)` for unmatched samples", and "pseudo-outputs (`gc`, `uncollapsible`) are omitted only when their aggregate is exactly zero; negative aggregates are signed data and must be reported". Here a row with aggregate 7 is silently dropped, so summary matching_time_ns=7 has no owning row — the same silent-data-loss class the spec's R2-07/R3-05 rules forbid. The validation paragraph enumerates name rules (duplicates, default booleans) and never reserves the pseudo names; grep for "reserved"/pseudo-name reservation across docs/CLANKERPROF*.md finds nothing, and goalloop.audit.md has no prior refutation on this (R3-05 was the separate negative-aggregate gating fix). So the behavior is undocumented magic contradicting the documented ownership/omission rules, identical-but-harmful in both languages.
Q1.subclaims: All auditor sub-claims held: (1) both implementations accept configs naming a slice `(gc)` or `(uncollapsible)` — exit 0, no validation error; (2) ordinary samples are attributed to those slices (slices.py:404 / slices.rs:259 store under the configured name); (3) rendering unconditionally removes those rows (render.py:912-914 / slices.rs:306-310); (4) summary reports matched time with no corresponding slice (matching_time_ns=7, matching_pct=100.0, slices=[] in all four runs); (5) validation covers duplicate/multiple-default names but never reserves the pseudo names (slices.py:344-358, slices.rs:202-222); (6) exit code 0 in both languages as claimed. Additional note beyond the auditor's claim: because both languages fail identically, this is a shared correctness defect, not a parity break — the parity suite cannot catch it.

Q2.evidence:
REPRODUCTION (worktree /Users/logan_martel/Projects/autoclanker_bootstrap_v5/clankerprof-v2-audit, HEAD of clankerprof-v2 audit tree). Fixture built with tests/fixtures/pprof_builder.py via .goalloop-support/triage6/Q2/build_fixture.py: three single-frame samples match_a@/a/one.rb +10, match_b@/b/two.rb -10, other_c@/c/three.rb +5; .goalloop-support/triage6/Q2/slices.yml defines slices A (paths [/a/**]), B (paths [/b/**]), default (default: true).

1) uv run clankerprof slices --profile .goalloop-support/triage6/Q2/profile.pb --slices .goalloop-support/triage6/Q2/slices.yml --filter name:match
   -> EXIT 0. Output (trimmed): slice "A" {"time_ns": 10, "pct": 200.0, frames: [{"function": "match_a", "pct": 200.0}]}, slice "B" {"time_ns": -10, "pct": -200.0, frames: [{"function": "match_b", "pct": -200.0}]}, "summary": {"matching_pct": 0.0, "matching_time_ns": 0, "total_time_ns": 5}.
2) cargo run -q -p clankerprof-core --bin clankerprof-rs -- slices --profile ... --slices ... --filter name:match
   -> EXIT 0, same numbers. Artifacts written with --output and diffed: BYTE_IDENTICAL (shared defect, not a parity break).

Every number in the auditor's reproduction is exact: matching_time_ns 0, total_time_ns 5, A and its frame pct 200.0, B pct -200.0, exit 0 in both CLIs. The only samples selected by the filter sum to 0, and the rendered percentages are shares of the unrelated unfiltered +5 sample.

DEFECT SITES. clankerprof/render.py:902 `total = result.matching_time_ns or result.total_time_ns`; crates/clankerprof-core/src/slices.rs:303-307 `let total = if result.matching_time_ns != 0 { result.matching_time_ns } else { result.total_time_ns };`. Every dependent pct site (render.py:939 slice pct + frame/library pcts; slices.rs:407, :437, :453, plus gc/uncollapsible at :380/:391) already implements the documented zero arm (`... if total else 0` / `if total == 0 { json!(0) }`), but the fallback makes it unreachable whenever total_time_ns != 0 — the zero arm only fires for a fully empty profile.

CONTRACT CITATIONS.
- docs/CLANKERPROF.md:477-497 (slices output example) pins the matching-time denominator arithmetically: with matching_time_ns 128000000 and total_time_ns 180000000, slice "components" time_ns 52000000 shows pct 40.6 (52/128 = 40.625; against total it would be 28.9) and its frame 25400000 shows 19.8 (25.4/128 = 19.84; against total 14.1); matching_pct 71.1 = 128/180. The example is only consistent with per-row pct = share of matching time.
- docs/CLANKERPROF_SPEC.md:329-331 (rendering rules): "A zero parent total (valid signed samples can cancel to exactly zero) renders every dependent percentage as `0` — never a division error, `inf`, or `NaN`" — the spec explicitly anticipates signed cancellation and mandates pct 0, not a denominator swap.
- docs/CLANKERPROF_SPEC.md:533-535 (scopes): estimates "suppressed only when the total is exactly zero (undefined share), mirroring the zero-arm of the pct fields" — the spec treats the zero-arm as the general property of pct fields across projections.
- Nothing documents the total-time fallback as deliberate: `grep -rn "denominator\|matching_time\|fallback" docs/*.md` yields only scopes parent-denominator prose and the output example; goalloop.audit.md has no prior finding or refutation touching the slice denominator. Prior confirmed R4-03 established the same principle for targets ("define zero-total pct as 0.0, both languages").

HARM AMPLIFIER: per docs/CLANKERPROF_SPEC.md compare contract, "Compare gates on the supplied percentage fields" — a cancellation-window slices report feeds ±200.0 pct values straight into the regression gate.
Q2.subclaims: 1. Both CLIs exit 0 with matching_time_ns 0, total_time_ns 5, A/frame pct 200.0, B pct -200.0 — HELD, reproduced exactly, byte-identical artifacts in both languages (B's frame is also -200.0, consistent with the claim). 2. "Contradicts the documented matching-time denominator" — HELD, with one caveat: no normative prose sentence names the slice pct denominator; the documentation is by worked example (docs/CLANKERPROF.md:477-497), whose numbers (40.6 = 52M/128M, 19.8 = 25.4M/128M) are only consistent with the matching-time denominator. 3. "The documented zero arm should not silently switch to the unrelated +5 sample" — HELD in substance: the spec's zero-arm rule (SPEC.md:329-331, written in the target rendering-rules section but generalized by SPEC.md:533-535 "mirroring the zero-arm of the pct fields") explicitly covers signed cancellation and mandates rendering pct as 0; both renderers implement that arm at every slice pct site, but the render.py:902 / slices.rs:303 fallback preempts it whenever the whole profile is nonzero. The fallback is documented nowhere as deliberate. 4. Cited file/line references — ACCURATE (render.py:902 fallback, :929/:939 dependent pct sites; slices.rs:303 fallback, :317 by-slice threshold on the swapped total, :407 slice pct; CLANKERPROF.md:479 the example summary). 5. Severity "major" — REASONABLE: pct is precisely the field the compare gate reads, so the arbitrary values are gate-relevant, and the by-slice %-threshold selection (render.py:929, slices.rs:317) also evaluates against the wrong denominator in the same case.
Q2.fix_sketch:
Direction: make the slice renderer honor the already-implemented zero arm instead of swapping denominators — one line per language, byte-parity preserved by construction.

Python — clankerprof/render.py:902:
  total = result.matching_time_ns
(delete the `or result.total_time_ns` fallback).

Rust — crates/clankerprof-core/src/slices.rs:303-307:
  let total = result.matching_time_ns;

Every dependent pct site in both languages already has the symmetric zero arm (Python `... if total else 0` emitting int 0; Rust `if total == 0 { json!(0) }`), so a cancelled matching total now renders slice/frame/library/gc/uncollapsible pct as `0` — matching the documented rule and the existing empty-profile precedent (`"matching_pct": 0`). Rows keep their signed time_ns (satisfies the negative-aggregates-must-render rule). The `--by-slice N%` guard (`if total and ...` / `total != 0 && ...`) already handles a zero total.

Blast radius is confined to the bug case: in slices.py all row/gc/uncollapsible accumulation happens after the filter gate (slices.py:381-383), so when filters match nothing there are no rows and the artifact bytes are unchanged; bytes change only when matched signed samples cancel to exactly zero — the case being fixed. `summary.matching_pct` is computed from total_time_ns directly and is untouched.

Docs: add one normative sentence to the slice projection contract (docs/CLANKERPROF_SPEC.md, "Slice projection contract" section ~line 536): "Slice, frame, library, and pseudo-output `pct` fields are shares of `matching_time_ns` (the filtered, attributed total; the whole-profile `total_time_ns` is reported in the summary only). A zero matching total (valid signed samples can cancel to exactly zero) renders every dependent percentage as `0` — never a silent fallback to the whole-profile total." Update the corresponding row in docs/CLANKERPROF_PARITY.md.

Tests: add the Q2 fixture (three samples +10/-10/+5, slices A/B/default, --filter name:match) as (a) a Python unit test in tests/test_clankerprof.py asserting pct == 0 for A/B with time_ns ±10 preserved, (b) a Rust unit test in crates/clankerprof-core/src/slices.rs, and (c) a byte-for-byte case in tests/test_clankerprof_rust_parity.py. Full behavioral parity is achievable here (pure integer/zero-arm logic; no dialect divergence), so no document-and-pin carve-out is needed.

Q3.evidence:
Spec contract (docs/CLANKERPROF_SPEC.md:526-530): "Scope totals for one scope must remain internally additive: rollup cost-kind rows sum to the scope total, and owner cost-kind rows sum to their owner totals. Zero-aggregate rows may be omitted from rendered rollups (they cannot affect the sums), but rows with negative aggregates must be rendered — sample values are signed, and dropping negative rows breaks additivity." Reinforced by docs/CLANKERPROF_PARITY.md:98: "Zero-aggregate rollup rows are omitted while negative rows render, keeping bucket/owner sums equal to scope totals byte-identically".

Fixture (built via tests/fixtures/pprof_builder.PprofFixtureBuilder, script at /Users/logan_martel/Projects/autoclanker_bootstrap_v5/clankerprof-v2-audit/.goalloop-support/triage6/Q3/build_fixture.py): samples (Pos,S)=+10, (Neg,S)=-10, (Unrelated)=+5. Config .goalloop-support/triage6/Q3/scopes.yml: cost_kind {Positive: "name:Pos", Negative: "name:Neg"}, scope function S with rollup Work: ["Positive","Negative"].

Python (from worktree root):
$ uv run clankerprof scopes --profile .goalloop-support/triage6/Q3/profile.pb --config .goalloop-support/triage6/Q3/scopes.yml ; echo exit=$?
{"boundaries": [{"attributable_estimates": {}, "buckets": [], "domains": [], "name": "S", "pct_of_profile": 0.0, "samples": 2, "total_time_ns": 0}], "summary": {"total_time_ns": 5, "unique_frame_count": 4}, "tool": "clankerprof_boundaries"}
exit=0

Rust:
$ cargo run -q -p clankerprof-core --bin clankerprof-rs -- scopes --profile ... --config ... ; echo exit=$?  → exit=0
$ diff py_out.json rs_out.json → BYTE-IDENTICAL to Python

The nonzero Negative (-10) and Positive (+10) category rows are absent: bucket Work sums to 0, and the bucket-level filter drops the whole subtree — clankerprof/render.py:188 `buckets = [bucket for bucket in buckets if cast(int, bucket["time_ns"]) != 0]` and crates/clankerprof-core/src/scopes.rs:699-702 `.filter(|bucket| time_ns_of(&bucket["time_ns"]) != 0)`. Category rows themselves are correctly filtered only on `stats.cpu_time != 0` (render.py:220-227, scopes.rs:735-743), so the negative row would render but for the parent's removal.

Control isolating the defect (same profile, Positive/Negative in separate buckets via scopes_split.yml):
$ uv run clankerprof scopes --profile ... --config .goalloop-support/triage6/Q3/scopes_split.yml
buckets: [{"name": "Plus", "time_ns": 10, "categories": [["Positive", 10]]}, {"name": "Minus", "time_ns": -10, "categories": [["Negative", -10]]}]
→ negative rows (even a whole negative bucket) render fine when not cancelled inside one bucket; only zero-sum co-bucketing erases them.

Not documented as deliberate: goalloop.audit.md contains no refutation covering zero-sum buckets; prior CONFIRMED findings R2-07 and R3-05 established the "only exactly-zero rows may be omitted; signed data must render" doctrine, and the sole pinned test (tests/test_clankerprof.py::test_clankerprof_scope_rollups_render_negative_costs_additively) uses +10/-5, i.e. a nonzero-sum bucket, leaving the cancellation case unpinned.
Q3.subclaims: (1) Reproduction — HELD: both implementations exit 0 and emit exactly the JSON the auditor quoted (buckets [], name S, samples 2, total_time_ns 0, summary total_time_ns 5), byte-identical across Python and Rust. (2) "+10 and -10 rows both disappear" — HELD: control run with the categories in separate buckets renders both (Minus bucket at -10 renders), proving the wholesale bucket-level drop is the cause. (3) "Violates the normative rule that negative aggregate rows must render" — HELD: SPEC 529 ("rows with negative aggregates must be rendered") is unconditional, and PARITY.md:98 claims negative rows render; the -10 Negative category row is a nonzero negative-aggregate row and is not rendered; the zero-omission license (SPEC 528) covers only the bucket row itself, justified by "(they cannot affect the sums)", and no doc licenses suppressing nonzero must-render children. (4) One nuance the auditor's framing overstates — the literal additivity sum is NOT broken in this instance (a zero-contribution subtree removed from a zero-total scope leaves bucket-sum == scope-total), so the violation is of the must-render clause and signed-data visibility rather than a sum mismatch; severity 'major' is still fair since signed data silently vanishes from reports that the compare gate consumes. Additionally noted: the analogous owner/domain filter (render.py `stats.cpu_time != 0` in the domains comprehension; scopes.rs:710) has the same hole — a zero-cancelling owner is dropped with its nonzero cost_kind children, which are not even individually zero-filtered.
Q3.fix_sketch:
Keep a bucket when it has any rendered (nonzero) category rows, instead of filtering on the summed time_ns; since category rows are already zero-filtered, "empty categories" is exactly the "entire subtree is zero" case the spec's omission license covers. Byte-parity is preserved because both languages change identically and tie-sorting (time_ns 0) is stable insertion-order in both (Python list.sort, Rust sort_by_key).

Python (clankerprof/render.py:188):
    buckets = [
        bucket
        for bucket in buckets
        if cast("list[object]", bucket["categories"])
    ]
(equivalently: `if cast(int, bucket["time_ns"]) != 0 or bucket["categories"]`).

Rust (crates/clankerprof-core/src/scopes.rs:699-702):
    let mut buckets: Vec<Value> = buckets
        .into_iter()
        .filter(|bucket| bucket["categories"].as_array().is_some_and(|rows| !rows.is_empty()))
        .collect();

Apply the same treatment to the owner/domain filter (render.py domains comprehension `if stats.cpu_time != 0`, scopes.rs:710 `.filter(|(_, stats)| stats.cpu_time != 0)`): keep a domain when stats.cpu_time != 0 OR any of its cost_kind aggregates is nonzero, so a zero-cancelling owner still renders its signed cost-kind rows (per SPEC 527 "owner cost-kind rows sum to their owner totals").

No numeric hazards: retained zero-sum buckets hit the existing `if total else 0` pct arms and `_scale_attributables` total==0 → {} guard, so no division errors.

Accompany with: (a) extend tests/test_clankerprof.py::test_clankerprof_scope_rollups_render_negative_costs_additively (or add a sibling) with the +10/-10 single-bucket cancellation case asserting both category rows render; (b) add the case to the Rust parity matrix; (c) sharpen SPEC 528 to "a row may be omitted only when its aggregate AND its entire rendered subtree are zero" and refresh the PARITY.md:98 row wording. Full behavioral parity is achievable here — no cross-engine dialect issue, no document-and-pin needed.

Q4.evidence:
Spec contract (docs/CLANKERPROF_SPEC.md:593-596, compare section): "Duplicate top-level row names (a slice name, or a boundary/bucket/category/domain row within one boundary) are a validation error in both implementations — projections never emit duplicates, and last-wins would make the gate order-dependent." Nothing in the spec, docs/CLANKERPROF_PARITY.md, or skills/clankerprof-operator reserves the rollup name `Other` or documents the collision as deliberate (spec:320 "place unmatched time in `Other`" is the targets contract; the scopes section never mentions the implicit leftover bucket at all). goalloop.audit.md's sole prior refutation (P9, tampered-report trust model) does not cover this — here both artifacts are genuine projection output.

Code: Python loader `_load_boundary_bucket` (clankerprof/cli.py:801-826) accepts any bucket name including `Other` (only cross-bucket category duplication is rejected); renderer `_render_boundary` (clankerprof/render.py:170-187) unconditionally appends `buckets.append(_render_boundary_bucket(boundary, "Other", leftover))` after the configured buckets. Rust mirrors both: crates/clankerprof-core/src/scopes.rs:676-698 (`render_boundary` implicit `Other` push) and :1278-1304 (rollup loader, no reserved-name check).

Reproduction (worktree root; fixture = scope T with LeafA=10ns → cost kind A, LeafB=5ns → cost kind B; config `cost_kind: {A: "name:LeafA", B: "name:LeafB"}`, `scope: [{function: T, rollup: {Other: [A]}}]`; files under .goalloop-support/triage6/Q4/):

1. `uv run clankerprof scopes --profile .goalloop-support/triage6/Q4/profile.pb --config .goalloop-support/triage6/Q4/scopes_other.yml --output ...py_scopes.json` → stdout `{"ok": true, "output": "...", "tool": "clankerprof_boundaries"}`, exit=0. Buckets in the artifact: `Other 10 66.66666666666666` and `Other 5 33.33333333333333` — two bucket rows named `Other` in one boundary.
2. `cargo run -q -p clankerprof-core --bin clankerprof-rs -- scopes ...` same flags → exit=0, and `cmp py_scopes.json rs_scopes.json` → BYTE-IDENTICAL (same duplicate rows).
3. `uv run clankerprof compare --before py_scopes.json --after py_scopes.json` → `{"error": "Duplicate Bucket row 'Other' in comparison input.", "ok": false}`, exit=2. Rust compare on the Rust artifact: identical envelope, exit=2 (compare.py:292 emits the message).

So a valid, accepted config makes both projections emit an artifact that the project's own compare gate rejects, directly falsifying the spec's "projections never emit duplicates" justification for the duplicate-row rejection. Genuine self-consistency defect, identical in both languages (parity itself is intact).
Q4.subclaims: 1) Loader permits a rollup named `Other`: HELD in both languages (no reserved-name check; exit 0). 2) Rendering independently appends an implicit `Other` bucket for leftover nonzero categories: HELD (render.py:186; scopes.rs:696-698). 3) Both scope commands exit 0 and emit two bucket objects named `Other` with times 10 and 5: HELD, artifacts byte-identical across Python and Rust. 4) Self-compare exits 2 with the exact envelope {"error": "Duplicate Bucket row 'Other' in comparison input.", "ok": false}: HELD in both implementations. 5) Violates the documented invariant "projections never emit duplicates" (spec:595): HELD — the invariant is stated as fact in the compare contract and no doc reserves `Other` or documents the collision as deliberate. Severity "major" is fair: a config the tools accept produces artifacts unusable with the project's own regression gate.

Q5.evidence:
CONTRACT: docs/CLANKERPROF_PARITY.md:18 — "`crates/clankerprof-core` reaches parity with every capability below through `tests/test_clankerprof_rust_parity.py`: byte-level artifact comparison across a per-subcommand flag matrix (... slices with filters/...)"; row at line 146: "YAML config for common slice options | covered". The R5-pinned dialect rows commit Python to serde_yaml's dialect (line 197: "Plain YAML scalar resolution follows serde_yaml's dialect ... table pinned in both engines"; line 198: "Explicit YAML tags follow serde_yaml semantics ... local `!name` tags fail closed with one shared message on every YAML surface"). No doc row, SPEC.md text, or goalloop.audit.md refutation mentions merge keys (grep for "merge key|apply_merge|yaml.org,2002:merge|<<" over clankerprof/, crates/, docs/, tests/ returns nothing), so the asymmetry is undocumented.

REPRO (worktree /Users/logan_martel/Projects/autoclanker_bootstrap_v5/clankerprof-v2-audit, HEAD f8991f6). Fixture: one sample worth 7 ns with leaf /a/x/work.py (.goalloop-support/triage6/Q5/profile.pb built via tests/fixtures/pprof_builder.py). Slices file .goalloop-support/triage6/Q5/slices_merge.yml = auditor's exact input:
  defaults: &defaults\n  paths: ["/a"]\nslices:\n  - name: A\n    <<: *defaults

1) uv run clankerprof slices --profile .goalloop-support/triage6/Q5/profile.pb --slices .goalloop-support/triage6/Q5/slices_merge.yml
   -> PY_EXIT=0; output slice: {"is_default": false, "name": "A", "pct": 100.0, "time_ns": 7}; summary {"matching_pct": 100.0, "matching_time_ns": 7, "total_time_ns": 7}
2) cargo run -q -p clankerprof-core --bin clankerprof-rs -- slices --profile ... --slices ... (same files)
   -> RS_EXIT=0; output slice: {"is_default": true, "name": "(all)", "pct": 100.0, "time_ns": 7}; same summary {"matching_pct": 100.0, ...}
3) diff py_out.json rs_out.json -> only lines 13-14 differ: "is_default": false/"name": "A" vs "is_default": true/"name": "(all)". Both artifacts claim 100% matching, so a compare gate keyed on percentages conceals the ownership flip.
4) Mechanism probe: uv run python -c 'parse_strict_yaml(<file>)' -> {'defaults': {'paths': ['/a']}, 'slices': [{'paths': ['/a'], 'name': 'A'}]} — Python expanded the merge (jsonio.py:172-182 strips YAML 1.1 resolvers for timestamp/value/bool/int/float but NOT tag:yaml.org,2002:merge; the construct_mapping override at jsonio.py:148-169 delegates to SafeConstructor.construct_mapping, whose flatten_mapping expands `<<`; the `tag:` multi-constructor makes the merge-tagged key construct as the string "<<" so it passes the strict key checks first). Quoted probe: parse_strict_yaml('a: 1\n"<<": 2') -> {'a': 1, '<<': 2} (only the plain merge-tagged spelling expands in Python). Rust: serde_yaml never applies merges (Value::apply_merge is opt-in and never called); `<<` is an ordinary string key that passes require_string_keys (crates/clankerprof-core/src/rules.rs:447) and falls into load_slices_file's metadata loop (crates/clankerprof-core/src/slices.rs:119 fn start, metadata loop ~166-190), so slice A has no path_patterns, matches nothing, and the built-in "(all)" default absorbs the sample. Same accepted input, different attribution, both silent — violates the byte-parity contract and the documented "Python follows serde_yaml's dialect" direction.
Q5.subclaims: All five sub-claims HELD: (1) Python expands YAML merge keys through PyYAML — held; mechanism is SafeConstructor.flatten_mapping reached via jsonio.py:169's super() call, with the merge implicit resolver left intact at jsonio.py:172-182. (2) Rust leaves `<<` as an ordinary key — held; serde_yaml resolves the alias but never applies the merge, and slices.rs's metadata loop silently swallows the `<<` entry as slice metadata. (3) Python exits 0 with "name":"A","is_default":false — held, exact output reproduced. (4) Rust exits 0 with fallback "name":"(all)","is_default":true — held. (5) Both claim 100% matching, concealing the ownership change — held; matching_pct is 100.0 in both artifacts and the diff is confined to name/is_default. Severity "major" is fair: an accepted config silently changes slice ownership between engines on every YAML surface (slices files, scope YAML configs, external rule packs all share these loaders). One nuance beyond the auditor's text: the divergence is merge-tag-scoped — a QUOTED "<<" key is a literal key in both engines (no divergence); only the plain `<<` spelling diverges.
Q5.fix_sketch:
Full behavioral parity IS achievable here (no document-and-pin compromise needed). Preferred fix — fail closed on merge keys in both engines with one shared message, matching the R5 local-tag precedent ("local tags fail closed with one shared message on every YAML surface"):

Python (clankerprof/jsonio.py):
1. Add "merge" to _REPLACED_RESOLVER_TAGS (line ~172) so `<<` never resolves to the merge tag and SafeConstructor.flatten_mapping can never expand it (flatten_mapping matches on the tag).
2. Add YAML_MERGE_KEY_MESSAGE = "YAML merge keys are not supported in clankerprof inputs." and in _StrictYamlLoader.construct_mapping (jsonio.py:148-169), after the isinstance(key, str) check: `if key == "<<": raise ValueError(YAML_MERGE_KEY_MESSAGE)`.

Rust (crates/clankerprof-core/src/rules.rs, require_string_keys at line 447 — the single funnel for all four YAML surfaces: rule packs, scopes.rs:885, slices.rs:123, main.rs:870):
  In the Mapping arm, after the is_string check: `if key.as_str() == Some("<<") { return Err(YAML_MERGE_KEY_MESSAGE.to_string()); }` with the same message constant.

Parity note driving the design: post-parse, serde_yaml cannot distinguish plain `<<` (merge intent) from quoted `"<<"` (literal key), while PyYAML can (tag). Byte parity therefore requires rejecting the literal string key "<<" in BOTH engines regardless of quoting — hence the string-level check on both sides rather than a Python tag-only check. Rejecting quoted "<<" keys is an acceptable cost: no legitimate clankerprof input uses that key.

Minimal alternative (silent, exact serde_yaml emulation): step 1 alone (add "merge" to _REPLACED_RESOLVER_TAGS) makes Python treat `<<` as an ordinary string key exactly like Rust — byte parity achieved — but the user's merge intent silently becomes a metadata entry and the slice loses its paths, so the fail-closed variant is the better fit for this codebase's fail-closed philosophy.

Accompanying: add a parity-matrix row to docs/CLANKERPROF_PARITY.md ("YAML merge keys (`<<`) rejected on every YAML surface with the shared message"), a Python test asserting the shared envelope on slices/scopes/rules surfaces, and a Rust-parity test pinning identical stderr/exit codes.

Q6.evidence:
CONTRACT. docs/CLANKERPROF_SPEC.md:151-156: "All JSON inputs are strict RFC 8259: the non-standard `Infinity`/`-Infinity`/`NaN` tokens are validation errors in both languages, and duplicate object member names are validation errors on every JSON input surface — never silent last-wins". clankerprof/jsonio.py:6-8 (module contract): "Every clankerprof JSON/YAML input goes through these loaders so both implementations fail closed on the same inputs." Grep for surrogate/UTF-16/lone/unpaired across docs/, clankerprof/, crates/, goalloop.audit.md finds NO documented asymmetry — the only surrogate mention is output-side (SPEC:668, "\uXXXX (surrogate pairs for astral code points)"). No prior refutation in goalloop.audit.md covers this. Note: RFC 8259's grammar technically permits an unpaired `\uD800` escape (its §8.2 calls the result unpredictable), so the violation is of the both-languages/fail-closed-on-same-inputs promise, not of RFC syntax.

REPRODUCTION (worktree root; fixture = round-1 facts.json with strings[0] "Leaf" replaced by the literal 6-byte escape \ud800; frame [1,1,0,1,1,false] takes name from strings[0]; fixtures under .goalloop-support/triage6/Q6/):
1. `uv run clankerprof slices --facts .goalloop-support/triage6/Q6/facts_surrogate.json` -> exit 0, stdout contains `"function": "\ud800"` (lone surrogate accepted and projected).
2. `cargo run -q -p clankerprof-core --bin clankerprof-rs -- slices --facts .goalloop-support/triage6/Q6/facts_surrogate.json` -> exit 2, stderr `{"error": "unexpected end of hex escape at line 1 column 336", "ok": false}` (auditor saw column 145 on their own fixture; column is the engine-specific location suffix, same message core).
3. Variant probes (same commands on facts_trailing.json `\udc00` and facts_double_lead.json `\ud800\ud800`): Python exit 0 both; Rust exit 2 with `lone leading surrogate in hex escape at line 1 column 335`/`column 341`. Valid pair `😀` (facts_pair_valid.json): both exit 0 — divergence is exactly the unpaired-surrogate domain.
4. Downstream harm: `uv run clankerprof slices --facts .../facts_surrogate.json --output .../py_report.json` -> exit 0, artifact contains `\ud800`; `clankerprof-rs compare --before py_report.json --after py_report.json` -> exit 2 `{"error": "unexpected end of hex escape at line 7 column 30", "ok": false}` while `uv run clankerprof compare` on the same pair -> exit 0. A Python-produced artifact is outside the Rust gate's readable domain.
5. YAML twin: slices config with `name: "s\ud800"` (double-quoted \u escape): `uv run clankerprof slices --facts facts_pair_valid.json --slices slices_surrogate.yml` -> exit 0; Rust -> exit 2 `{"error": "found invalid Unicode character escape code at line 2 column 15, while parsing a quoted scalar at line 2 column 11", "ok": false}`.
Q6.subclaims: (1) Python accepts a lone-surrogate escape in facts strings and projects it: HELD (exit 0, emits "function": "\ud800"). (2) Rust rejects the identical input, exit 2, "unexpected end of hex escape": HELD (column number differs from the auditor's because my rebuilt fixture differs in byte length; the spec itself treats location suffixes as engine-specific detail). (3) "The shared JSON/facts input domain is therefore not actually shared": HELD — violates jsonio.py's stated fail-closed-on-same-inputs contract and the spec's both-languages framing; no documented deliberate asymmetry exists. Beyond the auditor's claim, two aggravations verified: Python legitimately WRITES a report artifact (documented ensure_ascii serialization re-emits \ud800) that the Rust compare gate rejects while the Python gate accepts, and the identical divergence exists on the YAML config surface via double-quoted \uD800 escapes (PyYAML accepts, serde_yaml rejects). Severity "minor" is fair: obscure input, fail direction is accept-vs-reject (no false-green), but it is a genuine undocumented parity break.
Q6.fix_sketch:
Full parity by ACCEPTANCE is not achievable: Rust String/serde_json/serde_yaml cannot represent an unpaired UTF-16 surrogate (Rust strings are valid UTF-8 by construction), so Rust cannot be made to accept. Python CAN be made to reject — the fail-closed direction this codebase consistently chooses — so this is fixable, not a document-and-pin-divergence case. Python-only fix in clankerprof/jsonio.py, no Rust change:

JSON (parse_strict_json): add a lexical pre-scan of string literals before json.loads (alongside _check_json_depth), replicating serde_json's rules so the message CORES match byte-for-byte (suffix stays engine-specific, like the duplicate-key rule): for each `\uXXXX` with XXXX in D800..DBFF — next char not `\` => raise ValueError("unexpected end of hex escape"); `\` not followed by `u`, or `\uYYYY` with YYYY not in DC00..DFFF => raise ValueError("lone leading surrogate in hex escape"); a bare `\uXXXX` with XXXX in DC00..DFFF => raise ValueError("lone leading surrogate in hex escape") (empirically serde's core for lone trailing too). Valid pairs pass. A post-parse walk rejecting any str containing U+D800..U+DFFF is simpler and semantically equivalent (json.loads collapses valid pairs into astral chars) but cannot reproduce serde's two distinct cores, so the pre-scan is the byte-parity-respecting option. Raw (unescaped) surrogates cannot enter via files read as strict UTF-8.

YAML (parse_strict_yaml): PyYAML's double-quoted \uXXXX escapes are the only YAML ingress; reject scalars containing U+D800..U+DFFF in _StrictYamlLoader (e.g. wrap construct_scalar) with serde_yaml's core "found invalid Unicode character escape code".

Docs/tests: extend the strict-RFC-8259 paragraph (docs/CLANKERPROF_SPEC.md ~line 151) with one sentence: unpaired UTF-16 surrogate escapes are validation errors on every JSON and YAML input surface in both languages (message cores pinned; Rust strings cannot represent them, Python rejects to match); add parity fixtures (lone leading at end-of-string, lone trailing, double leading, valid astral pair accepted) to tests/test_clankerprof_rust_parity.py and a jsonio unit test; update docs/CLANKERPROF_PARITY.md matrix row. Side benefit: forecloses the latent UnicodeEncodeError if a lone surrogate ever reached the raw-UTF-8 facts serialization path.

Q7.evidence:
SPEC (docs/CLANKERPROF_SPEC.md:654-658): "A slice is a regression only when it exceeds both the configured absolute and relative thresholds and is within the focus set when one is provided. Focus sets come from `--focus-slices` for slice reports and `--focus-boundaries` (alias `--focus-scopes`) for scope reports; both take comma-delimited names and gate only the named rows while still reporting every row." Lines 720-722 add only last-occurrence-wins for repeated flags. No sentence anywhere (spec, docs/CLANKERPROF_PARITY.md:173,194, tests) requires supplied focus names to exist or documents zero-match acceptance as deliberate — the auditor's contract-gap sub-claim holds. Notably the spec's own rationale for threshold validation (compare.py:34-36, spec ~line 630) is "a non-finite threshold would silently disable gating" — a zero-match focus set silently disables gating the same way, so the current behavior contradicts the spec's stated fail-closed posture rather than being pinned by it. Prior rounds confirmed the same defect class (R1-01 fail-open gate, R4-13 negative-threshold "contract hole"); the sole compare refutation in goalloop.audit.md (P9, artifact trust model) does not cover this.

CODE: clankerprof/compare.py:207 `in_focus = not resolved.focus_slices or name in resolved.focus_slices` and :355-357 (focus_boundaries); crates/clankerprof-core/src/compare.rs:119 `let in_focus = options.focus_slices.is_empty() || options.focus_slices.contains(&name);` — plain membership tests, no existence validation in either language.

(3) "With --focus-slices typo, both exited 0 with has_regression:false and the hot row stable/delta_abs 40.0/delta_rel 400.0" — HELD byte-identically in both engines. (4) "No warning or validation error identifies that the focus set matched zero rows" — HELD: stderr empty, report JSON carries no indicator. Q8.evidence:
Spec contract (docs/CLANKERPROF_SPEC.md:686-690): "Every contracted failure — decode errors ..., missing or unreadable input files, malformed YAML/JSON/TOML configs and rule packs, ..., and option validation — exits `2` and prints a single `{"ok": false, "error": ...}` JSON envelope to stderr, never a traceback." Core-class contract (spec:340-345): "Core-class CSV files (packaged or `--core-classes` overrides) parse their first field with CSV semantics (Python `csv.reader` default dialect): ... — identical class sets in both implementations." No file in docs/, clankerprof/, crates/, or tests/ mentions a field-size limit (`grep -rni "field_size\|field limit" docs/ clankerprof/ crates/ tests/` → no hits), and goalloop.audit.md has no refutation on this topic (prior rounds R4-09/R5-09 both CONFIRMED Rust-side core-class CSV parse fixes toward csv.reader semantics).

Reproduction (fixture: profile.pb with one 1-ns sample, leaf `child` called by `parent`; big.csv = one record whose single field is "A"*131073, one byte over csv.field_size_limit()'s default of 131072 — confirmed via `uv run python -c "import csv; print(csv.field_size_limit())"` → 131072):

Python: `cd /Users/logan_martel/Projects/autoclanker_bootstrap_v5/clankerprof-v2-audit && uv run clankerprof targets --profile .goalloop-support/triage6/Q8/profile.pb --target parent --runtime ruby --core-classes .goalloop-support/triage6/Q8/big.csv` → exit 1, empty stdout, stderr is a raw traceback ending:
  File ".../clankerprof/categorize.py", line 29, in load_ruby_core_classes
    for row in csv.reader(handle):
  _csv.Error: field larger than field limit (131072)

Rust: `cargo run -q -p clankerprof-core --bin clankerprof-rs -- targets --profile .goalloop-support/triage6/Q8/profile.pb --target parent --runtime ruby --core-classes .goalloop-support/triage6/Q8/big.csv` → exit 0, normal report: {"parents": {"parent": {"categories": [{... "name": "Other", "pct": 100.0, "samples": 1, "time_ns": 1}], "total_time_ns": 1}}, "tool": "clankerprof_targets"}.

Root cause confirmed by code read: `csv.Error.__mro__` is (_csv.Error, Exception, BaseException, object) — it is neither a ValueError nor in `_contracted`'s catch tuple `(OSError, EOFError, zlib.error, yaml.YAMLError, OverflowError)` (clankerprof/cli.py:104), so it sails past both the wrapper and main()'s `except ValueError` (cli.py:1636). Rust's hand-rolled parser (crates/clankerprof-core/src/rules.rs:510, doc comment at :501 "Pinned empirically against csv.reader") imposes no length limit. The limit is not a *dialect* property (it is the module-level csv.field_size_limit guard), so the spec's "csv.reader default dialect" pin does not document it, and spec:344-345's "identical class sets in both implementations" is violated either way.

Fix-direction validation: an in-process probe (.goalloop-support/triage6/Q8/fix_probe.py) that sets `csv.field_size_limit(sys.maxsize)` before invoking `clankerprof.cli.main` with the identical arguments → exit 0 and stdout byte-identical to the Rust output (`cmp` clean).
Q8.subclaims: 1. Python exits 1 with a traceback ending `_csv.Error: field larger than field limit (131072)` on a 131073-char first field — HELD (reproduced at HEAD). 2. `_csv.Error` lies outside the contracted exception boundary — HELD (csv.Error subclasses Exception directly; not in _contracted's catch tuple at cli.py:104, not ValueError at cli.py:1636). 3. Rust accepts the same CSV, exit 0, emitting a normal parent/Other report with time_ns 1 — HELD (reproduced; rules.rs parser has no field limit). 4. This violates the documented contract — HELD twice over: spec:686-690 forbids tracebacks/exit-1 for input failures regardless of accept/reject stance, and spec:344-345 requires identical class sets in both implementations; the 128 KiB cap is documented nowhere as deliberate. 5. Severity "major" — reasonable: it is both a contract-boundary escape (traceback on user-supplied input) and a cross-engine accept/reject divergence, though the triggering input (a >128 KiB single CSV field) is unusual in practice.
Q8.fix_sketch:
Python-only fix; no Rust change (Rust's unbounded parser is the behavior that matches the spec's "identical class sets" plus "records are scanned across the whole payload" intent, and byte parity of the resulting report is already verified).

(a) clankerprof/categorize.py — lift the csv module's default cap around both loaders, save/restore since field_size_limit is process-global:

    _CSV_FIELD_LIMIT = 2**31 - 1  # portable C-long bound; Rust's parser is unbounded

    def load_ruby_core_classes(path: str | Path) -> frozenset[str]:
        values: set[str] = set()
        old_limit = csv.field_size_limit(_CSV_FIELD_LIMIT)
        try:
            with Path(path).open(newline="", encoding="utf-8") as handle:
                for row in csv.reader(handle):
                    ...  # existing body unchanged
        finally:
            csv.field_size_limit(old_limit)
        return frozenset(values)

    (same wrap in load_default_ruby_core_classes for symmetry; sys.maxsize also works on 64-bit but 2**31-1 avoids OverflowError on 32-bit C long platforms.)

(b) clankerprof/cli.py — defense in depth: add `csv.Error` to _contracted's catch tuple (`except (OSError, EOFError, zlib.error, yaml.YAMLError, OverflowError, csv.Error) as exc:`) so any residual csv-module error still lands in the exit-2 envelope instead of a traceback.

(c) docs/CLANKERPROF_SPEC.md:340-345 — extend the core-class bullet: fields of any length are accepted; neither implementation imposes the csv module's 131072-byte default field limit.

(d) tests — add a parity case (tests/test_clankerprof_rust_parity.py flag matrix or a focused test) with a >131072-char core-class field pinning identical accept behavior and byte-identical reports in both engines.

Full behavioral parity is achievable here (no dialect impossibility): the limit is a Python-side DoS default, not a CSV semantic, so lift-and-pin is the correct resolution rather than document-and-diverge.

Q9.evidence:
CONTRACT. docs/CLANKERPROF_PARITY.md:17-19: "`crates/clankerprof-core` reaches parity with every capability below through `tests/test_clankerprof_rust_parity.py`: byte-level artifact comparison across a per-subcommand flag matrix (... targets json/csv/simple-csv/text ...)". docs/CLANKERPROF_V2_REQUIREMENTS.md:118 (B3-04): "byte-identical CSV/simple-csv/text in the flag matrix ... render.rs mirrors Python csv.writer quoting + format_time". The only documented f64-operand asymmetry pin is PARITY.md:208 and it is scoped to percentages: "Percentage arithmetic uses f64-operand division in both languages ... pinned above 2^53" — nothing pins elapsed-time formatting, and grep over docs/*.md plus goalloop.audit.md refutations found no deliberate-asymmetry note for format_time.

CODE. clankerprof/patterns.py:43-44: `milliseconds = nanoseconds / 1_000_000` — exact arbitrary-precision int operand, single correctly-rounded division. crates/clankerprof-core/src/render.rs:10-11: `let milliseconds = nanoseconds as f64 / 1_000_000.0;` with `TimeNs = i128` (model.rs:10) — rounds the aggregate to f64 BEFORE dividing. Double rounding diverges above 2^53. Offline check: for n=10717268054699998221, Python-order ms=0x1.37e9de92557ffp+43 -> "178621134.24 min"; Rust-order ms=0x1.37e9de9255800p+43 -> "178621134.25 min".

REPRO (worktree root). Built .goalloop-support/triage6/Q9/profile.pb via tests/fixtures/pprof_builder.py: samples leaf_a=5000000000000000000 and leaf_b=5717268054699998221 (both <= i64::MAX, so valid pprof), each with stack [leaf, parent]; aggregate under `parent` = 10717268054699998221 (> i64::MAX, <= u64::MAX — valid per the R2-02/R2-04 precedent that u64-range totals are legal).
- `uv run clankerprof targets --profile .goalloop-support/triage6/Q9/profile.pb --target parent --format csv` -> exit 0, row: `parent,Other,10717268054699998221,178621134.24 min,100.00,2,2,2,...`
- `cargo run -q -p clankerprof-core --bin clankerprof-rs -- targets --profile ... --target parent --format csv` -> exit 0, row: `parent,Other,10717268054699998221,178621134.25 min,100.00,...`; diff: DIVERGED (single byte pair .24/.25).
- Same commands with `--format text` -> both exit 0; diff shows 3 diverging lines: "Total CPU time under this function: 178621134.24 min" vs ".25 min", the Other category row, and the TOTAL row.

This is the same defect class as confirmed R3-04 (goalloop.audit.md:37), whose fix (render.py:113-122 `_f64_ratio`, "Adopted from Rust as the shared contract in audit round 3 (R3-04)") covered percentage fields only and left format_time on exact-int division.
Q9.subclaims: All sub-claims held: (1) Python divides the exact integer while Rust rounds to f64 first — confirmed at patterns.py:43-44 vs render.rs:10-11; (2) the two sample values are valid signed-i64 and the aggregate 10717268054699998221 is a valid total under the current spec (u64-range totals legal since R2-02/R2-04; Rust TimeNs is i128) — confirmed; (3) both CSV commands exit 0 with the exact cited bytes `178621134.24 min` vs `178621134.25 min` — confirmed; (4) text output likewise diverges (.24 vs .25 in three lines) — confirmed; (5) this contradicts the explicit CSV/text byte contract (PARITY.md:17-19, REQUIREMENTS B3-04) — confirmed; no doc documents this as a deliberate asymmetry (PARITY.md:208 pins f64-operand division for percentage arithmetic only). Severity 'major' is fair: valid-input byte-parity break, same class as previously confirmed R3-04.
Q9.fix_sketch:
Full behavioral parity is achievable here (unlike regex-dialect findings) — one-line arithmetic op-order fix, following the R3-04 precedent of adopting Rust's f64-operand order as the shared contract. Python-only code change, in clankerprof/patterns.py format_time (line 44):

    # f64-operand contract (shared with the Rust core, see R3-04/_f64_ratio):
    # round the aggregate to f64 BEFORE dividing so CSV/text elapsed-time
    # fields stay byte-identical above 2**53 (identical below it).
    milliseconds = float(nanoseconds) / 1_000_000.0

(float(n) is round-nearest-even, matching Rust's `as f64` i128->f64 cast; the subsequent /60_000 and /1_000 divisors are exactly representable so the remaining ops already match, and the >= threshold comparisons are unaffected below 2**53.) Rust needs no change. Accompany with: (a) a parity-suite case (tests/test_clankerprof_rust_parity.py) using an aggregate > 2**53 exercising targets csv + text (goldens are Python-generated so no conflict); (b) extend the PARITY.md:208 row (or add a sibling row) to pin that elapsed-time formatting (`format_time`) also uses f64-operand division in both languages above 2**53.

Q10.evidence:
CONTRACT — docs/CLANKERPROF_SPEC.md:664-670: "Non-facts JSON artifacts, receipts, and error envelopes use Python's `json.dumps` lexical form in both implementations: … and CPython `repr` float spelling (`1e-06`, `1e+21`, integral floats keep `.0`). … These are byte contracts verified by the parity suite." docs/CLANKERPROF_PARITY.md:191 claims this "covered" ("Non-facts JSON artifacts, receipts, and envelopes use Python `json.dumps` lexical form (`\uXXXX` escapes, CPython float repr) byte-identically | covered | test_clankerprof_rust_lexical_json_matches_python, Rust `pyjson` unit tests"). grep of SPEC/PARITY/RUST docs for shortest/tie/half-even/dtoa/grisu/ryu shows NO documented float tie-break asymmetry, and goalloop.audit.md has no refutation covering it (R2-09/R3-04 are the prior float-lexical confirms that produced pyjson.rs).

NUMERIC CORE — `uv run python -c "pa=(float(5268900186379573)/float(400))*100; print(repr(pa))"` → `1317225046594893.2` (bits via hex: 0x1.2b8096bf75535p+50). Rust `rustc` scratch binary computing the identical expression: `{}` → `1317225046594893.3`, `to_bits()` → `4312b8096bf75535` — SAME f64, different spelling. Exactness: `Decimal(pa)` → `1317225046594893.25`; `float('...893.2')==pa and float('...893.3')==pa` → True True; 16 digits does not round-trip. So both 17-digit candidates are equidistant shortest round-trippers: CPython (Gay dtoa mode 0) breaks the tie round-half-even on the last decimal digit → `.2`; Rust flt2dec rounds half-away → `.3`. Control case 1317225046594893.75 (candidates .7/.8, half-even and half-up agree): Python `1317225046594893.8`, Rust `1317225046594893.8` — confirms divergence is exactly the even-lower-candidate midpoint class, contradicting the comment at crates/clankerprof-core/src/pyjson.rs:141 "// {:e} yields the same shortest round-trip digits CPython repr uses." The pyjson unit table (pyjson.rs:193-217) contains no midpoint-tie row, which is why the parity suite missed it.

END-TO-END at HEAD f8991f6 — built .goalloop-support/triage6/Q10/profile.pb via tests/fixtures/pprof_builder.py with two single-frame samples +5268900186379573 (PosLeaf) and -5268900186379173 (NegLeaf), total 400. `uv run clankerprof slices --profile .goalloop-support/triage6/Q10/profile.pb` → exit 0, lines 9/16: `"pct": 1317225046594893.2,` / `"pct": -1317225046594793.2,`. `cargo run -q -p clankerprof-core --bin clankerprof-rs -- slices --profile …` → exit 0, `"pct": 1317225046594893.3,` / `"pct": -1317225046594793.3,`. `diff py_slices.json rs_slices.json` → exactly those two lines differ, diff exit=1. Byte contract violated on valid input with both implementations exiting 0, exactly as the auditor claimed. (Nuance: both spellings parse back to the same f64, so downstream compare-gate SEMANTICS are unaffected; the violation is the normative byte contract itself.)
Q10.subclaims: (1) "Rust serializer assumes Rust scientific formatting supplies the same shortest digits as CPython" — HELD: pyjson.rs:141 states the assumption verbatim; it is false on exact decimal-midpoint ties. (2) "which is false" — HELD, but narrower than stated: Rust's flt2dec matches CPython on shortest LENGTH (both use mantissa-parity-inclusive round-trip bounds); the sole divergence class is the last-digit choice when the f64's exact decimal expansion sits exactly halfway between the two shortest candidates AND the lower candidate's last digit is even (.25-style midpoints; .75-style agree, verified). (3) Reproduction (+5268900186379573/-5268900186379173, total 400 → Python …893.2/…793.2, Rust …893.3/…793.3, both exit 0) — HELD byte-for-byte. (4) "even when both implementations computed the same f64" — HELD: identical bits 4312b8096bf75535/c312b8096bf753a5 (the R3-04 shared f64-operand arithmetic held; only serialization diverged). (5) "directly contradicts the required CPython repr spelling and byte parity" — HELD per SPEC:664-670 and PARITY.md:191; no doc pins this as deliberate, so the refute condition does not apply.

Q11.evidence:
DOCS (current HEAD, f8991f6): docs/CLANKERPROF_V2_REQUIREMENTS.md:51 (A2-08): "Rust CLI parses arguments strictly via clap, rejecting what Python rejects (item 23)" — one-directional; it never licenses Rust rejecting what Python accepts. docs/CLANKERPROF_SPEC.md:720-723: "`--focus-slices` and `--focus-boundaries` each take a single comma-delimited value; repeating a focus flag keeps the last occurrence in both implementations rather than accumulating." docs/CLANKERPROF_PARITY.md:194: "Focus flags take one comma-delimited value; a repeated flag keeps the last occurrence in both CLIs | covered". The last-wins contract is scoped to focus flags only; no doc mentions repeated ordinary scalars, and grep for "cannot be used multiple times" across docs/ and tests/ finds nothing pinning the Rust rejection as deliberate. (PARITY.md:143 "Duplicate scalar config and CLI options are rejected" is a different axis: the same option given in `slices --config` AND on the CLI — see tests/test_clankerprof.py:4231, message "show_paths specified both".) No prior refutation in goalloop.audit.md covers repeated CLI flags.

REPRO (worktree root, scratch under .goalloop-support/triage6/Q11 = $Q, empty.pb is a 0-byte file = valid empty pprof):
1. `uv run clankerprof facts --profile $Q/empty.pb --profile $Q/empty.pb` → exit 0, valid v2 facts ({"schema_version":"clankerprof.sample_facts.v2",...,"sample_count":0}).
2. `cargo run -q -p clankerprof-core --bin clankerprof-rs -- facts --profile $Q/empty.pb --profile $Q/empty.pb` → exit 2, envelope: {"error": "error: the argument '--profile <PROFILE>' cannot be used multiple times\n\nUsage: clankerprof-rs facts [OPTIONS] --profile <PROFILE>...", "ok": false}.
3. Python `compare --before $Q/report.json --after $Q/report.json --threshold-abs 1 --threshold-abs 2` → exit 0, valid compare report. Rust same invocation → exit 2, "the argument '--threshold-abs <THRESHOLD_ABS>' cannot be used multiple times".
4. Python is genuinely last-wins, not merely tolerant: `facts --profile <1-sample profile> --profile $Q/empty.pb` → sample_count 0; reversed order → sample_count 1.
5. The special-casing is real: `grep -n overrides_with crates/clankerprof-core/src/main.rs` → exactly 8 sites: 6x output, focus_slices (line 270), focus_boundaries (line 276). Rust repeated `--focus-slices a --focus-slices b` → exit 0; repeated `--output o1 --output o2` → exit 0, writes o2.json (last-wins). Everything else rejects.
6. Divergence extends beyond scalars to boolean flags: Rust `facts --profile $Q/empty.pb --pretty --pretty` → exit 2 "the argument '--pretty' cannot be used multiple times"; Python → exit 0.

VERDICT BASIS: Reproduced exactly as claimed; genuine parity defect on the "shared CLI surface" (SPEC's own framing) — identical invocations succeed in the reference implementation and exit 2 in Rust. The project demonstrably treats repeated-flag semantics as a parity axis (it standardized and pinned focus flags as last-wins in both), so the unstandardized remainder is undocumented divergence, not a documented deliberate asymmetry. Severity minor is fair: wrapper scripts that append override flags (a common pattern relying on GNU last-wins) work against Python and break against Rust.
Q11.subclaims: (1) "Python argparse keeps the last occurrence of ordinary scalar options" — HELD (proven last-wins with two different profiles: 0 vs 1 sample_count depending on order). (2) "Rust clap rejects duplicates except on specially annotated fields" — HELD (rejections on --profile/--threshold-abs/--pretty; overrides_with only on 6x output + 2 focus fields, all verified accepting last-wins). (3) Exact repro exit codes (Python 0/0, Rust 2/2 with the quoted clap message) — HELD verbatim at HEAD. (4) "Focus flags were explicitly standardized as last-wins, but the remaining scalar surface was not" — HELD (SPEC:720-723 and PARITY.md:194 scope last-wins to focus flags only; A2-08 is one-directional; no doc or test pins the broader Rust rejection as deliberate). Additional scoping beyond the auditor's claim: the same asymmetry also affects repeated boolean flags (--pretty), and the only intentionally accumulating option (--target) is consistent in both (argparse action="append" at clankerprof/cli.py:1373, Vec<String> at main.rs:77) — the fix must not touch it.
Q11.fix_sketch: Full behavioral parity is achievable here — no dialect impasse; adopt last-wins everywhere, matching the focus-flag precedent (last-wins is argparse's native behavior and the conventional GNU semantics wrapper scripts rely on; making Python reject duplicates instead would require a custom argparse action across the entire surface and contradict the already-pinned focus-flag resolution). Rust-only code change, in crates/clankerprof-core/src/main.rs: give every non-accumulating option self-override semantics, i.e. add `overrides_with = "<field>"` to each scalar Option/PathBuf/String field not already annotated (profile, facts, config, runtime, runtime_rules, core_classes, semantic_callers_csv, cpu_attributables, format, target_csv_layout, threshold_abs, threshold_rel, top, unattributed_libraries, by_slice(if scalar), slices/scopes config paths, etc.) and to each bool flag (pretty, no_enhanced, fold_runtime_internals, verbose_runtime_internals, track_semantic_callers, show_paths, no_collapse_native, allow_virtual_attribute_slices, ...), e.g. `#[arg(long, overrides_with = "pretty")] pretty: bool`. Leave Vec fields (targets --target; slices --filter/--collapse/--attribute) untouched — they accumulate identically in both. `overrides_with` composes fine with `required = true` and `visible_alias`. Doc changes: generalize docs/CLANKERPROF_SPEC.md:720-723 to "repeating any non-accumulating option (scalar or boolean) keeps the last occurrence in both implementations; only the documented accumulating options (--target, --filter, --collapse, --attribute) append"; update the PARITY.md:194 row to the generalized statement and add coverage. Tests: extend the Rust flag-matrix parity test with a repeated-scalar + repeated-bool invocation pinned byte-identical to Python, plus a Python-side test asserting exit 0 last-wins for repeated --profile/--threshold-abs (pins argparse behavior against regression).

Q12.evidence:
Code at HEAD (branch clankerprof-v2, worktree /Users/logan_martel/Projects/autoclanker_bootstrap_v5/clankerprof-v2-audit):
- Python clankerprof/cli.py:997-1004 (`_load_boundary_options`): `slices_path = payload.get("slices")`; `if slices_path is not None: raw_slices_path = Path(str(slices_path))` — any present value is str()-coerced and treated as a file path.
- Rust crates/clankerprof-core/src/scopes.rs:1353 (`load_boundary_options`): `if let Some(slices_path) = payload.get("slices").and_then(Value::as_str)` — a present non-string value is silently treated as absent.

Reproduction (fixture: F8 profile, one Leaf->T sample worth 7 ns; scope config .goalloop-support/triage6/Q12/scopes_bad_slices.yml with `slices: 123`, cost_kind AppWork=name:Leaf, scope function T label S):
- `uv run clankerprof scopes --profile .goalloop-support/triage6/Q12/profile.pb --config .goalloop-support/triage6/Q12/scopes_bad_slices.yml` → stdout `{"error": "[Errno 2] No such file or directory: '.goalloop-support/triage6/Q12/123'", "ok": false}`, exit 2. (The auditor's `/dev/fd/123` Bad-file-descriptor variant is the same code path with a process-substitution config whose parent dir is /dev/fd.)
- `cargo run -q -p clankerprof-core --bin clankerprof-rs -- scopes --profile ... --config ...` → exit 0, ordinary-looking report: `"tool": "clankerprof_boundaries"`, boundary `"name": "S"`, `"total_time_ns": 7` — the `slices: 123` field is ignored entirely.

Contract: docs/CLANKERPROF.md (scopes section, ~line 275) documents the field as a slice-file path: "load a slice file with `slices = \"./slices.yml\"`". docs/CLANKERPROF_SPEC.md (scope-config validation, ~lines 382-395) sets the norm for exactly this config surface: "A scope's `count` must be the string `occurrence` or `once_per_sample`; any other value (including non-strings) is a validation error, never a silent occurrence default." and "A scope's `label` and `name` values must be strings ... other shapes are validation errors in both implementations, because Python `str()` and Rust `Display` spell non-string scalars differently." Nothing in CLANKERPROF_SPEC.md, CLANKERPROF_PARITY.md, or goalloop.audit.md documents the Rust leniency on `slices` as deliberate (the parity doc's documented divergences cover regex engine edges only; the sole prior refutation, R5 compare-percentages, is unrelated). Prior rounds confirmed the identical pattern as defects: R2-10 ("Rust scope config validation silently substitutes defaults") and R3-06 ("non-string values coerced/dropped divergently ... Fix: reject non-strings, identical messages, both languages"). This is a genuine parity + fail-closed defect: the same invalid config exits 2 in Python and produces a clean exit-0 report in Rust.
Q12.subclaims: (1) Python treats a present `slices` value as a path and fails — HELD (exit 2; though note it fails via str() coercion into a misleading OS file error, not a proper type-validation message, so Python is only accidentally fail-closed). (2) Rust uses `as_str` and silently treats every non-string as absent — HELD (scopes.rs:1353, verified by exit-0 run). (3) Invalid configuration can produce an ordinary-looking Rust report — HELD (boundary S, total_time_ns 7, tool clankerprof_boundaries). (4) Cited file/line anchors — all four accurate at HEAD. Severity "minor" is fair: it needs an invalid config, but it silently disables ownership-slice loading, which can matter when `slice:` predicates are absent (with them, Rust would still error later via the "slice: predicates ... require slices" check).
